name: shoulder tap push-up
instructions: Start in a high plank position with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart and your body in a straight line from head to heels.
Lower your body towards the ground by bending your elbows, keeping them close to your sides.
As you push back up, lift your right hand off the ground and tap your left shoulder.
Return your right hand to the ground and repeat the push-up, this time lifting your left hand and tapping your right shoulder.
Continue alternating shoulder taps with each push-up repetition.
Maintain a stable core and avoid excessive hip rotation throughout the exercise.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

Start in a high plank position with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart and your body in a straight line from head to heels. Lower your body towards the ground by bending your elbows, keeping them close to your sides. As you push back up, lift your right hand off the ground and tap your left shoulder. Return your right hand to the ground and repeat the push-up, this time lifting your left hand and tapping your right shoulder. Continue alternating shoulder taps with each push-up repetition. Maintain a stable core and avoid excessive hip rotation throughout the exercise. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
